---
title: "Week8-Ways to gate"
author: "Macarena López-Mayorga"
date: 2026-06-11
format: pdf
---
First, we have to check if we have all the necessary packages installed and the proper versions:

```{r}
packageVersion("ggplot2")
```
[1] ‘4.0.3’

```{r}
packageVersion("ggcyto")
```
[1] ‘1.39.4’

```{r}
packageVersion("openCyto")
```
[1] ‘2.22.0’

```{r}
packageVersion("flowGate")
```
[1] ‘1.10.0’

This version of flowGate seems to have problems with shiny app because there are some bugs not resolved. David Rach and his colleagues have developed a forked version that fix the bug problems. So, I am going to remove flowGate package and install the version of David Rach and others:

```{r}
remove.packages("flowGate")
```
 Now, I have to call the remotes packages to install from the github repository

```{r}
library(remotes)
```

And install the forked version:
```{r}
install_github("DavidRach/flowGate")
```
Let's check now if we have the correct version:

```{r}
packageVersion("flowGate")
```
[1] ‘0.99.2’

Yes! that is correct :) . Now, we have to load in several packages that will be necessary:

```{r}
library(flowWorkspace)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggcyto)
library(flowGate)
library(openCyto)
```

```{r}
StorageLocation <- file.path("C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data")
fcs_files <- list.files(StorageLocation, pattern=".fcs", full.names=TRUE)
fcs_files
```
[1] "C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data/2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_Ctrl.fcs"
[2] "C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data/2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_SEB.fcs" 
[3] "C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data/2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_Ctrl.fcs"
[4] "C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data/2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_SEB.fcs" 
[5] "C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data/2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_Ctrl.fcs"
[6] "C:/Users/Macarena/CytometryInR/course/08_WaysToGate/data/2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_SEB.fcs" 

```{r}
SFC_cytoset <- load_cytoset_from_fcs(fcs_files,
 truncate_max_range = FALSE, transformation = FALSE)
SFC_GatingSet <- GatingSet(SFC_cytoset)
```

```{r}
SFC_Parameters <- colnames(SFC_GatingSet)
FluorophoresOnly <- SFC_Parameters[!stringr::str_detect(SFC_Parameters, "FSC|SSC|Time")]

Biexponential <- flowjo_biexp_trans(channelRange=4096, maxValue=262144,
     pos=4.5, neg=2, widthBasis=-500)
MyBiexTransform <- transformerList(FluorophoresOnly, Biexponential)
transform(SFC_GatingSet, MyBiexTransform)
```
A GatingSet with 6 samples

```{r}
ggcyto(SFC_GatingSet[1], subset="root",
 aes(x="BUV805-A", y="BV650-A")) + geom_hex(bins=100)
```
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/ggplot2 1.png")

So far, we have stablished a set of files (cytoset), set a GatingSet and applied the necessary transformations. Now...
# Manual-flowGate
David Rach usually starts with singlets

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="singlets", sample=1,
dims=list("FSC-A", "FSC-H"), subset="root")
```
The main arguments we need to specify values for are: the name of our GatingSet (gs); provide a name for our new gate (filterId); designate which sample in the Gating Set to use for plotting (sample=1); provide the X and Y fluorophore within a list style argument (dims=list(“FSC-A”, “FSC-H”)); and finally the subset to be used as the parent gate. Given we haven’t drawn any gates at this point, we will specify the “root” gate.
Once we run the chunk (or the codes, in the console), appears the shiny app. We have to select polygon and we should draw the gate of interest. We will see the line when we click for the second time. When we have finished, then, we have to click in "done". This should be the message that we will receive:

Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6323
done!
$Gate
Polygonal gate 'singlets' with 13 vertices in dimensions FSC-A and FSC-H

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

To check our gates within our GatingSet:
```{r}
plot(SFC_GatingSet)
```
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/plot gates.png")
The gate has been created and applied to our GatingSet. A question that may arise, was this gate applied to just one specimen, or to all specimens in the GatingSet? Let’s go ahead and check!

```{r}
gs_pop_get_count_fast(SFC_GatingSet)
```
                                  name Population Parent Count ParentCount
                                <char>     <char> <char> <int>       <int>
1: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_Ctrl.fcs  /singlets   root  9824       10000
2:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_SEB.fcs  /singlets   root  9862       10000
3: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_Ctrl.fcs  /singlets   root  9864       10000
4:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_SEB.fcs  /singlets   root  9755       10000
5: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_Ctrl.fcs  /singlets   root  9750       10000
6:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_SEB.fcs  /singlets   root  9790       10000

The gate appears to have been applied across the board to all specimens, since we have counts for the respective gate for all the specimens.
Let’s continue, and add another gate, this time for viability dye Zombie NIR, to determine what cells are alive. We will need to modify the arguments we provide, since this will be a new gate, based on new parameters, and placed under the singlets gate.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="live", sample=1,
dims=list("FSC-A", "Zombie NIR-A"), subset="singlets")
```
As you might notice, the included .fcs files look to have already been pre-processed, as there are few dead cells. Glancing at the y-axis, based on what we saw during Week 06 we can also deduce that behind-the-scenes the ggcyto/ggplot2 plot is not using coords_cartesian(), as it has zoomed in to the full extent of the data that is present.
This time, we use the rectangle. When you have done, you have to click in done.

Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6323
done!
$Gate
Rectangular gate 'live' with dimensions:
  FSC-A: (887796.37385814,2359987.5)
  Zombie NIR-A: (1534.1608886719,2727.4569994262)

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

```{r}
plot(SFC_GatingSet)
```
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/plot gates 2.png")

And validate by checking to see if the statistics output has changed

```{r}
gs_pop_get_count_fast(SFC_GatingSet) |> 
    dplyr::filter(Population %in% "/singlets/live")
```
                                  name     Population    Parent Count ParentCount
                                <char>         <char>    <char> <int>       <int>
1: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live /singlets  9421        9848
2:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live /singlets  9709        9899
3: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live /singlets  9203        9870
4:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live /singlets  9159        9778
5: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live /singlets  9610        9838
6:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live /singlets  9677        9841

To use the span tool, we are going to gate live T cells.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="Tcells", sample=1,
dims=list("Spark Blue 550-A", "Spark Blue 574-A"), subset="live")
```
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6482
done!
$Gate
Rectangular gate 'Tcells' with dimensions:
  Spark Blue 550-A: (3025.2558319391,3789.8569335938)

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

```{r}
plot(SFC_GatingSet)
```
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/plot gates 3.png")

```{r}
gs_pop_get_count_fast(SFC_GatingSet) |> 
    dplyr::filter(Population %in% "/singlets/live/Tcells")
```

                                  name            Population         Parent Count ParentCount
                                <char>                <char>         <char> <int>       <int>
1: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells /singlets/live  9416        9421
2:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells /singlets/live  8867        9709
3: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells /singlets/live  9132        9203
4:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells /singlets/live  8509        9159
5: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells /singlets/live  9330        9610
6:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells /singlets/live  8337        9677

Having gated down to T cells, lets go ahead and add a few gates for the individual T cell populations. Let’s start by drawing a polygon gate around CD4+ T cells.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="CD4+", sample=1,
dims=list("BV650-A", "BUV805-A"), subset="Tcells")
```
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6482
done!
$Gate
Polygonal gate 'CD4+' with 15 vertices in dimensions BV650-A and BUV805-A

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

Also, in case anyone was curious, flowGate retains ggcyto arguments, so if we wanted to, instead of fluorophore names, we could use alternatively use the marker names when specifying the axis for the flow cytometry plots. 
For example: 
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="CD4+", sample=1,
dims=list("CD8", "CD4"), subset="Tcells")
We can also modify the setting to overlay already existing gates when we are drawing new gates for the other two T cell subsets. This is done by providing the gate name to the overlayGates argument.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="CD8+", sample=1,
dims=list("CD8", "CD4"), subset="Tcells", overlayGates="CD4+")
```
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6482
done!
$Gate
Polygonal gate 'CD8+' with 14 vertices in dimensions BV650-A and BUV805-A

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

And we can repeat this for CD4-CD8- (Double Negative (DN)) T cells.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="DN", sample=1,
dims=list("CD8", "CD4"), subset="Tcells", overlayGates=c("CD4+", "CD8+"))
```
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6482
done!
$Gate
Polygonal gate 'DN' with 14 vertices in dimensions BV650-A and BUV805-A

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

And a final-check to make sure the gates for the T cell subsets were added

```{r}
plot(SFC_GatingSet)
```
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/plot gates 4.png")

And a quick check for the statistics

```{r}
gs_pop_get_count_fast(SFC_GatingSet) |> 
    dplyr::filter(Population %in% "/singlets/live/Tcells/DN")
```
                                  name               Population                Parent Count ParentCount
                                <char>                   <char>                <char> <int>       <int>
1: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells/DN /singlets/live/Tcells   593        9416
2:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF052_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells/DN /singlets/live/Tcells   625        8867
3: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells/DN /singlets/live/Tcells   244        9132
4:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF100_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells/DN /singlets/live/Tcells   231        8509
5: 2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_Ctrl.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells/DN /singlets/live/Tcells   265        9330
6:  2025_07_26_AB_02_INF179_00_SEB.fcs /singlets/live/Tcells/DN /singlets/live/Tcells   275        8337

The last type of manual gate that we can implement with flowGate is a quadrant gate, in this case, let’s use it to create a quadrant for our two cytokines (TNFa and IFNg). We should ideally use one of the SEB-stimulated specimens for this if we want to visualize the cytokine-expressing cells, so I have switched over to the 6th sample in the GatingSet.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="Cytokines", sample=6,
dims=list("TNFa", "IFNg"), subset="CD4+")
```
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6482
done!
$Gate
Quadrant gate 'Cytokines' with dimensions:
  PE-Dazzle594-A: 2833.933
  BV750-A: 2898.905

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

```{r}
plot(SFC_GatingSet)
```
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/plot gates 5.png")

## Redraw Gate

But what happens if we misdraw a gate and need to correct it? If you are in the process of actively drawing, you can hit the reset button and try again. But what if you had already created the gate?

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="CD161+", sample=1,
dims=list("CD161"), subset="CD8+")
```
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:6482
done!
$Gate
Rectangular gate 'CD161+' with dimensions:
  BV480-A: (2141.1587347718,3679.5215289787)

$Bins
[1] 256

$Scaling
[1] "unused"

![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/regate 1.png")
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/regate 2.png")

To completely redraw an existing gate, you would need to add the “regate” argument and set it to TRUE. This will delete the existing gate, and allow you to draw a new one in its place.

```{r}
flowGate::gs_gate_interactive(gs=SFC_GatingSet, filterId="CD161+", sample=1,
dims=list("CD161"), subset="CD8+", regate=TRUE)
```

 Select the new gate:
![]("C:/Users/Macarena/MacarenaLM/notes/week8-ways to gate/regate 2.png")
